title: Proteins
paperName: Paper 1 Multiple Choice & Paper 2 AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Cambridge 9700 Paper 2 — Proteins (2.3). A-Level Notes diagram-backed lesson with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Describe the general structure of an amino acid and the formation/hydrolysis of peptide bonds.
Explain the four hierarchical levels of protein structure (primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary) and the bonds/interactions that stabilise each.
Compare and contrast fibrous and globular proteins, relating their specific three-dimensional structures to their diverse biological functions.

simpleExplanation.title: Proteins: The Workhorses of Life
simpleExplanation.summary: Proteins are incredibly versatile macromolecules, built from smaller units called amino acids linked into long chains. Their unique 3D shapes dictate the astonishing variety of jobs they perform, from forming structures in your body to speeding up crucial chemical reactions. Think of them as the tiny, highly specialised molecular machines essential for life.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a protein as a highly intricate LEGO® model. Each LEGO brick is an amino acid. The specific way these bricks are connected, folded, and assembled into a unique 3D structure determines whether you've built a robust bridge (fibrous protein) or a complex robot (globular protein), each with a different purpose. A tiny change in how the bricks connect can completely change the model's function.
steps: Master the basic structure of an amino acid and how peptide bonds form.
Visualise how the primary sequence leads to secondary, tertiary, and sometimes quaternary structures.
Understand the different types of bonds stabilising each structural level.
Connect the final 3D shape (fibrous vs. globular) to the protein's specific biological function.

sections.content: Welcome to your premium revision on **proteins**! These incredible **macromolecules** are the **workhorses of life**, performing an astonishing array of functions in every living organism. From catalysing reactions as **enzymes** to providing structural support and transporting substances, understanding proteins is fundamental to Cambridge Biology 9700. Let's master their intricate structures and diverse roles, preparing you for both Paper 1 and Paper 2. Key points align with **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** 9700 Biology chapter **2** (topic **2.3**). Notes and diagrams from **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** chapter **2**, topic **2.3** (3 pages).

At the heart of every protein lies the **amino acid**, a monomer with a distinct general structure. Each amino acid contains a central carbon atom (the **alpha-carbon**) bonded to four different groups: an **amino group** (-NH₂), a **carboxyl group** (-COOH), a hydrogen atom (-H), and a unique side chain known as the **R-group** (or variable group). It's this R-group that gives each of the 20 common amino acids its specific chemical properties, influencing how a protein folds and interacts.

Amino acids link together to form long chains called **polypeptides**. This crucial process involves a **condensation reaction** (also known as dehydration synthesis). The carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another, releasing a molecule of water and forming a strong **peptide bond** between the carbon and nitrogen atoms. The reverse reaction, **hydrolysis**, uses water to break the peptide bond, releasing individual amino acids – a process vital during digestion.
Amino Acid₁ + Amino Acid₂ ⇌ Dipeptide + Water (Condensation/Hydrolysis)

The journey from a simple amino acid chain to a functional protein is one of intricate folding, driven by various interactions. Proteins exhibit four hierarchical levels of structure, each contributing to their final, biologically active 3D conformation.

items: a molecule made up of many amino acids linked together by peptide bonds is a polypeptide.
polypeptides can be broken down to amino acids by breaking the peptide bonds in a hydrolysis reaction.

this happens naturally in the stomach and small intestine during digestion Structures of proteins Image: https://www.khanacademy.org/ Bonds in the tertiary structure Image: By WikiComTD - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=79148318 PROTEINS GLOBULAR FIBROUS spherical/balled shaped.
curl up so that their non- polar, hydrophilic R- groups point to the centre of the molecule away from watery surroundings.
made of 4 polypeptide chains therefore they have a quaternary structure.
2 of the haemoglobin chains, ⍺ -chains, are made of ⍺ - globin.
the other 2 chains, β-chains, are made of β-globin.
each polypeptide chain has a haem group attached (prosthetic group) to it.
haem contains charged particle of iron.
the haem group is also responsible for the colour of haemoglobin.
each polypeptide chain can carry one molecule of oxygen.
therefore, in total, haemoglobin can carry 4 molecules of oxygen or 8 oxygen atoms Collagen: a fibrous protein.
found in skin, tendons, cartilage, bone, teeth, etc.
collagen molecule consists of 3 polypeptide chains, each in a helical shape Image: https://rosemarycottageclinic.co.uk/.
helical polypeptides are wound together creating a triple helix.

sections.content: **Examiner Tip:** Be precise when describing bonds at different levels. Hydrogen bonds are key for secondary structure (backbone interactions) and tertiary structure (R-group interactions). Disulfide bridges are *covalent* and thus much stronger, found in tertiary and quaternary structures, often dictating stability. Remember, *all* levels are ultimately determined by the primary sequence.
Proteins can broadly be classified into two main categories based on their overall shape and function, arising directly from their unique 3D structures:

**Fibrous Proteins:** These are typically long, insoluble polypeptide chains that are organised into parallel fibres or sheets, giving them great tensile strength. They have structural roles and are often repetitive in their amino acid sequence. Examples include **collagen** (in connective tissues, bones) and **keratin** (in hair, nails).

**Globular Proteins:** These proteins have a compact, spherical shape, often soluble in water. Their complex tertiary and sometimes quaternary structures allow them to form specific active sites or binding sites, making them ideal for metabolic and functional roles. Examples include **enzymes** (like amylase), **hormones** (like insulin), and **haemoglobin**.

sections.question: Explain how the primary structure of a protein determines its tertiary structure and ultimately its function. (3 marks)
sections.solution:
1. The primary structure is the specific linear sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain.
2. This sequence dictates the R-groups present and their specific positions, which then determine the types and locations of interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, disulfide bridges, hydrophobic interactions) that form during the folding process.
3. These interactions lead to the unique and specific three-dimensional tertiary structure. This precise 3D shape, particularly the presence of an active site or binding site, is essential for the protein to carry out its specific biological function (e.g., enzyme catalysis, hormone binding).

sections.content: **Examiner Tip:** When comparing fibrous and globular proteins, focus on their solubility, shape, and general function. Fibrous = structural, insoluble, long. Globular = metabolic/functional, soluble, spherical. Always link back to the 3D structure dictating these properties.

faq.q: What are proteins and why are they important in biology for Cambridge 9700?
faq.a: Proteins are large, complex macromolecules made from amino acid subunits. They are vital for nearly all biological processes, acting as enzymes, structural components, transporters, and signalling molecules. Understanding their structure and function is crucial for success in Cambridge 9700 Biology, covering topics from cell biology to genetics. Explore our free lessons and past paper guides to master this topic!

faq.q: How do amino acids link together to form a polypeptide chain?
faq.a: Amino acids link via a **condensation reaction** between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another, forming a **peptide bond** and releasing a water molecule. This process builds the polypeptide chain, which then folds into a functional protein. Practice identifying these bonds and reactions in past paper questions available on our site.

faq.q: What is the difference between primary and tertiary protein structure?
faq.a: **Primary structure** is the linear sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, determined by genetic code. **Tertiary structure** is the overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide chain, formed by folding of secondary structures and stabilised by interactions between R-groups. The primary structure dictates the tertiary structure. Our premium lessons explain these levels in detail and offer worked examples to solidify your understanding for 9700 exams.
